coredata.dc:description: The dysfunction of acetylcholinesterase (AChE) activity is responsible for many complex nervous diseases, demonstrating the urgent demand of constructing assays with robust performance for determining AChE activity. Herein, a ratiometric fluorescent (RF) sensing platform for AChE activity was strategically fabricated, which was based on the self-assembly of Ag+-modified Au nanoclusters and silicon nanoparticles (Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs) nanohybrids to perform as the RF probes. The Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs nanohybrids were facilely prepared through the electrostatic attraction between positively charged SiNPs and negatively charged Ag-AuNCs. AChE can catalyze the hydrolysis of ATCh into thiocholine (TCh) that in turn quenched the fluorescence of Ag-AuNCs in the Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs nanohybrids. Meanwhile, the fluorescence emission intensity of SiNPs remained stable. Consequently, based on the change of RF signal, AChE activity was sensitively detected in the range of 0.03–4.0 U/L with a low detection limit of 0.0021 U/L. This RF strategy was applied for AChE activity sensing in human serum sample with reliable performance, indicating the vast application prospect of this assay for the AChE-related clinical investigation.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagrams of the construction of Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs nanohybrids and the ratiometric fluorescent system for the sensing of AChE activity.

Characterizations of the prepared Ag-AuNCs and SiNPs. TEM images of Ag-AuNCs (A) and SiNPs (C). Inserts displayed their corresponding size distribution histograms. UV–vis, FL excitation and FL emission spectra of Ag-AuNCs (B) and SiNPs (D). Inset showed their pictures under sunlight (left) and 365 nm UV light illumination (right).

(A-C) TEM images of Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs nanohybrids at different magnifications. The DLS results (D), RLS spectra (E), XRD patterns (F) and FT-IR spectra (G) of Ag-AuNCs, SiNPs and Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs nanohybrids, respectively.

(A) FL emission spectra of SiNPs, Ag-AuNCs and Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs nanohybrids, respectively. (B) FL emission spectrum of SiNPs and UV–vis absorption spectrum of Ag-AuNCs. (C) FL lifetime decay curves of SiNPs and SiNPs in Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs nanohybrids, respectively.

The FL intensity of SiNPs and Ag-AuNCs in Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs nanohybrids and the corresponding fluorescence ratio (R = FAg-AuNCs/FSiNPs) under different pH (A), NaCl concentration (B) and radiation time of xenon lamp (C). (D) FL emission spectra of Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs nanohybrids after storage for different times at 4 °C.

(A) FL emission spectra of Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs, AChE/Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs, ATCh/Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs and AChE/ATCh/Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs system, respectively. (B) UV–vis spectra of Ag-AuNCs with the attendance of AChE, ATCh and ATCh/AChE, respectively. FL emission spectra of AuNCs (C) and Ag-AuNCs (D) after the addition of AChE, ATCh and AChE/ATCh, respectively.
(A) FL emission spectra of the AChE/ATCh/Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs system with varied concentrations of AChE. (B) The plot of R0-R versus AChE concentration in the range 0.03-8.0 U/L.

(A) The ﬂuorescence emission intensity ratio (R = FAg-AuNCs/FSiNPs) of the ATCh/Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs system with AChE or different interfering substances. (B) The ﬂuorescence emission intensity ratio (R = FAg-AuNCs/FSiNPs) of the AChE/ATCh/Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs system with different interfering substances. 100 μM ions, 10 μg/mL CHO and HSA, 50 μM and other biomolecules, 8.0 U/L AChE, 40 U/L other bio-enzymes.

head.ce:title: Constructing self-assembled nanohybrids for the ratiometric fluorescent sensing of acetylcholinesterase activity

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A ratiometric fluorescent platform was ingeniously constructed for the sensitive analysis of AChE activity by employing the Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs nanohybrids as the probes for the first time.

The dysfunction of acetylcholinesterase (AChE) activity is responsible for many complex nervous diseases, demonstrating the urgent demand of constructing assays with robust performance for determining AChE activity. Herein, a ratiometric fluorescent (RF) sensing platform for AChE activity was strategically fabricated, which was based on the self-assembly of Ag+-modified Au nanoclusters and silicon nanoparticles (Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs) nanohybrids to perform as the RF probes. The Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs nanohybrids were facilely prepared through the electrostatic attraction between positively charged SiNPs and negatively charged Ag-AuNCs. AChE can catalyze the hydrolysis of ATCh into thiocholine (TCh) that in turn quenched the fluorescence of Ag-AuNCs in the Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs nanohybrids. Meanwhile, the fluorescence emission intensity of SiNPs remained stable. Consequently, based on the change of RF signal, AChE activity was sensitively detected in the range of 0.03–4.0 U/L with a low detection limit of 0.0021 U/L. This RF strategy was applied for AChE activity sensing in human serum sample with reliable performance, indicating the vast application prospect of this assay for the AChE-related clinical investigation.

ce:section.ce:para: Acetylcholinesterase (AChE), acting as a critical bio-enzyme in the central nervous system, plays an essential role in biological signal transmission. The rapid hydrolysis of neurotransmitter acetylcholine (ACh) by AChE can terminate the transmission of nerve impulses at cholinergic synapses [1]. The aberrant expression of AChE triggered low level of ACh is closely associated with diverse neurodegenerative diseases, such as Parkinson’s disease [2], Alzheimer’s disease [3], and Huntington’s disease [4]. Accordingly, developing new drugs that can inhibit the AChE-regulated ACh hydrolysis to increase the ACh concentration creates many promising symptomatic treatment options for neurological diseases [5]. Besides, the AChE activity has been regarded as the characteristic marker for organophosphate poisoning and clinical diagnosis of cancer. For example, the AChE activity in some primary tumor tissues and serum samples from cancer patients has been confirmed to increase [6]. Based on the above considerations, sensitive and effective detection of AChE activity is imperative for AChE-related new drug discovery and early disease diagnosis.

Until now, diverse analytical techniques for AChE activity have been developed, such as colorimetry [7], electrochemistry [8], fluorimetry [9], and liquid crystal [10]. Especially, fluorescent methods have gained intensive interests benefitting from their operation simplicity, remarkable sensitivity and response speediness [11,12]. Regrettably, most of the previous fluorescent assays are on the basis of the single fluorescent emission signal mode. These single fluorescence-based assays may be subjected to the incapability of the highly accurate detection of AChE activity, which is as a result of the potential disturbances by various potential factors, such as the variation of probe concentrations and the fluctuation of environment conditions. Conversely, by employing two or more distinguishable fluorescent emission signals as reporters, ratiometric fluorescent (RF) methods emerge to effectively surmount these above-mentioned drawbacks, achieving the selective, reliable and accurate assay of AChE activity [13,14]. Several ingenious RF strategies have been proposed for the determination of AChE activity. For instance, by integrating the single atomic Fe anchored on N-enriched porous carbon (Fe-SAs/NC) and Cu nanoclusters (CuNCs), our group constructed an RF platform for AChE activity sensing based on the transformation of o-phenylenediamine to fluorescent 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP) through the Fe-SAs/NC-mediated catalytic oxidation and DAP performing as the fluorescence quencher for CuNCs [15]. Similarly, based on the fluorescence quenching of nitrogen-doped carbon dots by the oxidation product DAP, Zhang’s group fabricated an RF strategy to evaluate the AChE activity [16]. By employing MnO2 nanosheets as the fluorescence quencher, Guo et al. designed a graphene quantum dots/o-phenylenediamine-based RF probe for AChE analysis [17]. Regretfully, the requirement of pre-prepared fluorescence quenchers and the photo-oxidation of o-phenylenediamine may be detrimental to the operational simplicity and the accuracy of the analysis results. Therefore, the construction of no fluorescence quencher-involved RF strategies for the AChE activity sensing with simplicity and robust sensing performance is still urgently desired.

Compared with traditional fluorescent dyes, fluorescent nanomaterials have been popularized as the remarkable materials for sensing owing to their advantages of superior optical properties, simple preparation strategies and regulable size and morphology [18–20]. Among these multifarious fluorescent nanomaterials, silicon nanoparticles (SiNPs), emerging as the typical zero-dimensional Si nanomaterial, are greatly promising in biosensing applications attributed to their ultrahigh stability, low toxicity and satisfactory water solubility [21–23]. On the other hand, in the merits of large Stokes shift, good biocompatibility, ultra-small size, high quantum yield and long lifetime, fluorescent Au nanoclusters (AuNCs) emerge as the ideal alternatives in the research area of catalysis, oncological science, bioimaging and biosensing [24–27]. Especially, skillfully constructing Ag+-modified AuNCs (Ag-AuNCs) to promote the fluorescent intensity of AuNCs and the sensitivity of the AuNCs-based strategy for biosensing has become an attractive research direction, such as the Ag-AuNCs-based systems for the ultra-sensitive assay of ions [28,29]. In view of these remarkable advantages of SiNPs and Ag-AuNCs, the integration of them to construct RF platforms is of wide prospect in the field of biosensing. However, currently, the application of Ag-AuNCs and SiNPs for RF sensing is still scarce. Therefore, developing a suitable strategy to construct Ag-AuNCs and SiNPs-based RF sensing systems and exploring their biosensing applications are of high research significance.

Enlightened by the above merits, herein, the Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs nanohybrids were strategically constructed through a facile and fast self-assembly method, and the Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs nanohybrids-based RF platform was rationally designed for the sensitive biosensing of AChE activity without any additional fluorescence quencher for the first time (Scheme 1 ). As described in Scheme 1A, SiNPs with abundant amino group can electrostatically connect with Ag-AuNCs that possessed negatively charged surface due to the deprotonation of the carboxyl group on Ag-AuNCs surface. Significantly, the Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs nanohybrids can be used as RF probes for detecting AChE activity (Scheme 1B). AChE can efficiently catalyze the hydrolysis of acetylthiocholine (ATCh) to produce thiocholine (TCh) that can effectively quench the Ag-AgNCs fluorescence in the Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs nanohybrids. Meanwhile, SiNPs in the Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs nanohybrids remained unchanged to perform as the reference signal. Based on the fluorescence ratio of this platform, an RF method was constructed for the AChE activity sensing with robust performance. More importantly, the analysis of AChE in human serum samples by this RF strategy was successfully demonstrated with high accuracy.
Reagents and apparatus were displayed in the supplementary material.

Initially, glutathione-stabilized AuNCs were prepared by referring to the previous report [30]. In brief, 0.6 mL glutathione (100 mM) and 4.0 mL HAuCl4 (10 mM) were mixed with 15.4 mL H2O under stirring for 5 min at 25 °C. Subsequently, the above solution was heated to 70 °C and kept for 24 h with stirring. The obtained AuNCs solution was further processed by the dialysis procedure through a dialysis bag (MWCO 1000 Da) for 24 h.

Following that, Ag+-modified AuNCs (Ag-AuNCs) were fabricated based on our previous work [31]. In a typical experiment, 2 mL AgNO3 (10 mM) was injected into 40 mL AuNCs solution. The mixture was incubated at 25 °C for 1 h with stirring to prepare Ag-AuNCs. The Ag-AuNCs solution was purified through a dialysis bag (MWCO 1000 Da) for 12 h to get rid of the free Ag+. At last, the Ag-AuNCs solution was preserved at 4 °C for utilization.

The protocol for the preparation of fluorescent SiNPs was carried out by a convenient one-step hydrothermal route [32]. In a typical synthesis, 1 mL 3-aminopropyl triethoxysilane (APTES) and 10 mL ascorbic acid (100 mM) were added into 4 mL ultrapure water under stirring at 25 °C. After 10 min, the above mixture was moved to a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated to 240 °C for 180 min. Subsequently, the obtained solution was processed by a dialysis bag (MWCO 3500 Da) in ultrapure water for 8 h.

For preparing Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs nanohybrids, 1 mL Ag-AuNCs and 2.5 mL SiNPs were simply mixed together. After equilibrium for 5 min at 25 °C, the nanohybrids were obtained and utilized for the following assay.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection procedure for AChE activity
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical procedure of quantitatively detecting AChE activity, 50 μL AChE with varied activities and 30 μL ATCh (1 mM) were injected into 220 μL HEPES buffer (pH 7.4, 10 mM) and the mixture was incubated at 37 °C for 50 min. Afterwards, 100 μL Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs nanohybrids, 450 μL NaAc-HAc buffer (pH 6.5, 100 mM) and 150 μL H2O were added into the above mixture and incubated for 10 min. Finally, the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded under the excitation of 350 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: AChE activity analysis in human serum sample
ce:section.ce:para: Human blood sample from healthy adult volunteers was contributed by the Jilin University China-Japan Union Hospital (Jilin, China). The blood sample was pretreated for obtaining serum sample as following: the sample was filtered through the 0.45-μm filters and then processed by centrifugation at the rotate speed of 12,000 r/min for 20 min to get rid of the particulate matters. The spiked serum samples were prepared by mixing the serum with varied amounts of AChE. The analysis of AChE in the serum samples was performed by the described procedures in section 2.5.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterizations of Ag-AuNCs and SiNPs
ce:section.ce:para: In the synthesis of Ag-AuNCs, AuNCs were firstly prepared by using glutathione as the reducer and template through a convenient one-pot synthetic strategy [30]. Fig. S1 showed that the AuNCs exhibited good mono-dispersity with the average diameter of 2.08 nm. And the Ag-AuNCs were obtained by the modification of glutathione-templated AuNCs by Ag+ according to our previous work [31]. As revealed in Fig. S2, the fluorescence emission intensity of AuNCs displayed a remarkable enhancement after the Ag+ modification to prepare Ag-AuNCs. In our previous investigation, the silver in Ag-AuNCs was found to consist of Ag+ and Ag0, and the mechanism of silver-induced enhancement of AuNCs fluorescence was mainly attributed to the Ag+ anchored on the AuNCs shell and the Ag0 that existed on the AuNCs core structure can remove the surface defect and affect the luminescent feature of AuNCs [31]. Besides, SiNPs were prepared by a one-step hydrothermal method employing ascorbic acid as the reductant and 3-aminopropyl triethoxysilane as the silicon source in the water phase [32]. First of all, the morphologies and optical properties of Ag-AuNCs and SiNPs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) and fluorescence (FL) spectra. Fig. 1 A showed the obtained Ag-AuNCs were well dispersed in the spherical shape with a narrow size distribution, and the average diameter of Ag-AuNCs was about 1.93 nm. UV–vis spectrum of Ag-AuNCs exhibited a slight shoulder at 400 nm, and a strong and sharp fluorescence emission peak of Ag-AuNCs was observed at 600 nm under 367 nm excitation (Fig. 1B). The large stokes shift of Ag-AuNCs over 200 nm can efficiently avoid interference between the fluorescence excitation and emission signals. Fig. 1B insert depicted that the Ag-AuNCs exhibited light yellow in solution under daylight, and the orange fluorescence was emitted from the synthesized Ag-AuNCs under UV irradiation (365 nm). Besides, Fig. 1C showed the typical TEM images of SiNPs, in which uniform and dispersive dot-like nanoparticles can be observed with the average particle diameter size of 5.74 nm. Fig. 1D showed that 350 nm and 438 nm were the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of SiNPs, respectively. Besides, a characteristic absorption peak located at 270 nm was observed from the UV–vis spectrum of the SiNPs (Fig. 1D). The light yellow SiNPs solution displayed strong blue fluorescence under UV irradiation (365 nm) (Fig. 1D insert).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and characterizations of Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs nanohybrids
ce:section.ce:para: The Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs nanohybrids were simply fabricated by connecting SiNPs and Ag-AuNCs through the electrostatic interactions without the attendance of any other linker (Scheme 1A). TEM, zeta potential, resonance light scattering (RLS), dynamic light scattering (DLS), X-ray diffraction (XRD), FT-IR, fluorescent spectroscopy and lifetimes were conducted to systematically study the self-assembly process and physicochemical properties of Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs nanohybrids.

Zeta potentials were firstly recorded to demonstrate the formation of Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs nanohybrids by the electrostatic interactions. As revealed in Fig. S3, Ag-AuNCs were negatively charged with the zeta potential of −15.3 mV, which was due to the deprotonation of the carboxyl group of glutathione on the Ag-AuNCs surface. Meanwhile, the zeta potential of SiNPs was +37.2 mV attributed to the protonation of amino group on the SiNPs surface. The existences of carboxyl group and amino group were proven in the following Fig. 2 G. After assembling Ag-AuNCs with SiNPs, the zeta potential of Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs nanohybrids were +12.5 mV, which clearly verified the occurrence of electrostatic interactions between the negatively charged Ag-AuNCs surface and positively charged SiNPs surface.

TEM characterizations were conducted to directly exhibit the morphology of Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs nanohybrids. As depicted in the TEM images of Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs nanohybrids with different magnifications (Fig. 2A-C), compared with the monodisperse morphology in Fig. 1A and C, Ag-AuNCs and SiNPs showed apparent aggregation after forming Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs nanohybrids. DLS results in Fig. 2D showed the average hydrodynamic diameters (HDD) of Ag-AuNCs and SiNPs were around 2.52 nm and 5.91 nm, respectively. However, the average HDD of Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs nanohybrids largely increased to about 125.5 nm, which was also consistent with the TEM images and suggested that the Ag-AuNCs and SiNPs can self-assemble into large well-designed Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs nanohybrids. Besides, Fig. 2E exhibited the resonance light scattering (RLS) intensity of Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs nanohybrids was greatly enhanced compared with the individual Ag-AuNCs and SiNPs, further suggesting that the particle size gradually increased after forming Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs nanohybrids.

In addition, XRD and FT-IR characterizations were performed to characterize Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs nanohybrids. XRD patterns in Fig. 2F showed Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs nanohybrids exhibited two peaks at 2θ of 22° and 32° that matched with the (101) plane of silicon oxide in SiNPs [32] and (111) plane of gold in Ag-AuNCs [33], respectively, further implying the self-assembly of Ag-AuNCs and SiNPs to form Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs nanohybrids. Fig. 2G described the FT-IR spectra of Ag-AuNCs, SiNPs and Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs nanohybrids. The Ag-AuNCs exhibited the typical stretching vibrations of OH at 3410 cm−1 and CO stretching vibrations of carboxyl groups at 1647 and 1381 cm−1 [34,35], indicating the existence of carboxyl groups on the Ag-AuNCs surface. Typically, SiNPs displayed the sharp absorption peak at 1123 cm−1 corresponding to the Si-O-Si bonding vibrational stretch [36,37] and two strong characteristic peaks at around 1582 and 3363 cm−1 attributed to NH stretching vibration and NH bending vibration [38], which successfully demonstrated the existence of abundant amino groups on the SiNPs surface. The FT-IR spectrum of Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs nanohybrids basically showed the above-mentioned typical characteristic peaks of Ag-AuNCs and SiNPs with slight shifts of the peak positions, suggesting the successful achievement of Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs nanohybrids.

The fluorescence property of Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs nanohybrids was studied by recording the fluorescence spectra. As revealed in Fig. 3 A, after assembling Ag-AuNCs and SiNPs to fabricate Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs nanohybrids, the fluorescence emission intensity of SiNPs in the nanohybrids obviously decreased about 18 % compared with free SiNPs, while the fluorescence emission intensity of Ag-AuNCs in the nanohybrids enhanced about 39 % compared with free Ag-AuNCs. UV–vis spectra and fluorescence lifetimes were further collected to investigate mechanism of fluorescence changes of Ag-AuNCs and SiNPs after assembling. Due to the overlap between the SiNPs fluorescence emission spectrum and the Ag-AuNCs absorption spectrum (Fig. 3B) and the observed close distance between SiNPs and Ag-AuNCs in the TEM images (Fig. 2A-C), the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) can occur due to Ag-AuNCs and SiNPs fulfilling the basic principle requirements of spectral overlapping and the distance between donor and acceptor (< 10 nm) for constructing the FRET donor/acceptor pair [39–41]. In order to verify the FERT mechanism, the fluorescence lifetimes of SiNPs alone or in the form of Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs nanohybrids were determined. In general, the fluorescence lifetime of the donor in the FRET-based assembly will decrease with the acceptor addition [42,43]. As revealed in Fig. 3C and Table S1, the fluorescence lifetime of SiNPs obviously decreased after assembling with Ag-AuNCs, which further indicated that the assembly of Ag-AuNCs and SiNPs induced the occurrence of FRET from the energy donor of SiNPs to the acceptor of Ag-AuNCs to result in the fluorescence quenching of SiNPs and increase of Ag-AuNCs.

Besides, it has been previously reported that the metal nanoclusters possess the aggregation-induced emission enhancement (AIEE) property [44,45]. In particular, the AIEE property of glutathione templated metal nanoclusters has been skillfully utilized for various sensing applications [46–48]. Herein, the TEM images in Fig. 2A-C clearly showed that the glutathione capped Ag-AuNCs obviously aggregated after interacting with SiNPs. The AIEE property of Ag-AuNCs was verified by recording the fluorescence emission spectra of Ag-AuNCs after adding varied amounts of Ce3+, which was based on the reports that the Ce3+ can trigger the aggregation of glutathione capped metal nanoclusters through the coordination interaction between Ce3+ and the carboxylate terminals in the composing amino acids (glycine and glutamic acid) of glutathione [49,50]. As revealed in Fig. S4, the fluorescence emission intensity of Ag-AuNCs was greatly enhanced after the addition of Ce3+ from 0 to 4 μM, clearly suggesting the AIEE property of Ag-AuNCs. Therefore, the fluorescence increase of Ag-AuNCs was supposed to partially attribute to the FERT effect between SiNPs and Ag-AuNCs and partially originate from the self-assembled nanohybrids triggered AIEE property of Ag-AuNCs.

The stability of the nanohybrids was explored under varied pH, ionic strength, xenon lamp radiation and storage time. As revealed in Fig. 4 A, the fluorescence intensity of SiNPs and Ag-AuNCs in Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs nanohybrids and the corresponding fluorescence ratio (R = FAg-AuNCs/FSiNPs) changed less at pH 4.0−9.5. Fig. 4B showed that there was no obvious change in fluorescence intensity and R even at 500 mM salt concentration. As described in Fig. 4C, the fluorescence intensity and R had no significant decrease under the continuous radiation by a xenon lamp for 60 min, manifesting the excellent photostability of Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs nanohybrids. Besides, Fig. 4D displayed that the Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs nanohybrids can remain the fluorescence property with slight fluorescence intensity changes after being stored at 4 °C for 30 days, suggesting that Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs nanohybrids exhibited outstanding storage stability. These results demonstrated the favorable stability of Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs nanohybrids, effectively guaranteeing the potential application of Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs nanohybrids in biosensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design of ratiometric fluorescent platform for AChE
ce:section.ce:para: The fabricated Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs nanohybrids were employed as the RF probes for analyzing AChE activity. As described in Scheme 1B, with the AChE present, thiocholine (TCh) was generated through the AChE-modulated catalytic hydrolysis of acetylthiocholine (ATCh). TCh, as a kind of mercaptan molecule, can effectively quench the Ag-AuNCs fluorescence in the Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs nanohybrids, at the same time, the fluorescence emission of SiNPs in the Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs nanohybrids remained constant. As a result, the AChE activity can be directly determined by measuring the change of fluorescence intensity ratio (FAg-AuNCs/FSiNPs). As expected, Fig. 5 A showed that after the addition of individual ATCh or AChE, the fluorescence emission spectra of the Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs nanohybrids remained unchanged. However, after the addition of AChE and ATCh, the fluorescence emission intensity of Ag-AuNCs displayed obvious decrease, while the fluorescence emission intensity of SiNPs was not influenced, which clearly demonstrated the feasibility of this RF platform for the determination of AChE activity.

The mercaptan molecules have been studied to quench the fluorescence of metal nanoclusters via the strong metal-thiol interaction [51]. And in this work, UV–vis spectra were monitored to verify the combination between Ag-AuNCs and the hydrolysate TCh of AChE. As exhibited in Fig. 5B, the absorption of Ag-AuNCs had no change with ATCh and AChE addition. However, obvious decrease in the absorption peak around 400 nm of Ag-AuNCs was observed in the presence of AChE/ATCh, indicating the generated TCh can interact with Ag-AuNCs to change the surface electron energy of Ag-AuNCs [52]. As the exploration in our previous work [31], after Ag+ modification, most of Ag+ combined with the carboxylate groups on the AuNCs, and the modification of AuNCs by Ag+ can greatly enhance the fluorescence of AuNCs (Fig. S2). Besides, it has been reported that the mercaptan molecules have the strong ability to combine with Ag+ to remove the carboxylate-binding Ag+ [53]. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching effect of TCh on the AuNCs without the Ag+ modification was also conducted for comparison. As revealed in Fig. 5C, the hydrolysate TCh of AChE triggered 11 % fluorescence quenching of AuNCs. Based on the previous research of Yu et al. [54], the fluorescence quenching of AuNCs by TCh was due to the reduction of Au in AuNCs by the -SH of TCh. Obviously, 55 % fluorescence quenching of Ag-AuNCs by TCh was observed in Fig. 5D, indicating the Ag+ modification of AuNCs greatly enhanced the response sensitivity to AChE. Based on the above investigations, the hydrolysate TCh of AChE caused fluorescence quenching of Ag-AuNCs was mostly attributed to the capture of carboxylate-binding Ag+ by TCh through Ag-S bond, which resulted in the detachment of Ag+ from the Ag-AuNCs surface to restrain the Ag+ induced fluorescence enhancement of AuNCs.

Moreover, the pH and incubation time for TCh triggered fluorescence quenching of Ag-AuNCs in Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs nanohybrids were studied. As described in Fig. S5A, the fluorescence emission intensity ratio (R = FAg-AuNCs/FSiNPs) reached the minimum value at pH 6.5, suggesting that produced hydrolysate TCh in the AChE/ATCh system exhibited the strongest quenching capacity on the Ag-AuNCs fluorescence at pH 6.5. Fig. S5B showed that the ﬂuorescence emission intensity ratio (R) quickly declined and achieved the stable value at 10 min. As a result, pH value of 6.5 and reaction time of 10 min were selected for incubating hydrolysate TCh with Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs nanohybrids.

The pivotal factors of the incubation time for AChE and the concentration of ATCh were firstly explored to construct a highly sensitive analytical system for the AChE activity sensing. As depicted in Fig. S6A and Fig. S6B, the difference of fluorescence emission intensity ratio (R0-R, R and R0 were the fluorescence intensity ratio (FAg-AuNCs/FSiNPs) of AChE/ATCh/Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs system in the presence and absence of AChE) exhibited the maximum value under the conditions of enzyme incubation time of 50 min and ATCh concentration of 30 μM.

Under these optimized conditions, the capability of this RF analytical system for the quantitative assay of AChE activity was carried out. As shown in Fig. 6 A, the fluorescence emission intensity of Ag-AuNCs gradually decreased, whereas the emission of SiNPs exhibited negligible changes with increasing the concentration of AChE from 0 to 8.0 U/L. The difference of fluorescence emission intensity ratio (R0-R) was found to be linear with the concentration of AChE in the range from 0.03–4.0 U/L (Fig. 6B) with a calibration equation of R0-R = −0.0023 + 0.263CAChE, U/L (R2 = 0.992). The detection limit (LOD) for AChE activity was calculated to be 0.021 U/L (3σ/k, n = 11) [55].

Compared with other reports for AChE activity analysis, the LOD of our RF strategy was lower than most of those works (Table S2), suggesting the high sensitivity of this method for qualifying AChE activity. Additionally, over the past years, fluorescence method stands out among the various analytical technologies for AChE activity analysis. Especially, the RF strategy with outputting dual fluorescence signals possesses the self-calibration correction for environmental background effects, highly increasing the sensing reliability and accuracy for AChE activity. Until now, the researchers have purposefully designed several RF strategies for AChE activity analysis [15–17,56]. These RF sensing approaches were mainly based on the additional assistance of fluorescent quenchers and the conversion of non-fluorescent substance, such as o-phenylenediamine, to its fluorescent product to perform as the fluorescent signal reporter. And this conversion was realized through the oxidation of substance by auxiliary natural peroxidase or artificial peroxidase mimic. However, the non-fluorescent substance is usually unstable to be easily oxidized by the light, resulting in the obstacle for the accuracy of measurement. Besides, the requirement of auxiliary natural peroxidase obviously raised the analytical cost. Also, the complicated synthesis procedure of artificial mimics and quencher significantly increased the difficulty of experimental operation. Satisfactorily, compared with these RF strategies, our strategically constructed method achieved the specific response to AChE based on the hydrolysate of AChE directly triggered fluorescence signal variation without the attendance of any fluorescent substances, greatly improving the selectivity and sensitivity for AChE activity analysis. More importantly, this assay successfully avoided these previous RF methods involved synthetic materials and commercial enzyme, which largely simplified the experiment procedures and reduced the test cost to provide a highly selective, robust, convenient and economic alternative method for AChE activity analysis.

Selectivity and anti-interference are vital parameters to assess the analytical performance of the established strategies. Herein, the influences of substances of common electrolytes and biological species in actual samples, including ions (violet color), bio-enzymes (green color), amino acids and other biomolecules (orange color) were investigated (represented in different colors in Fig. 7 A). As illustrated in Fig. 7A, specificity experiments showed that the fluorescence emission intensity ratio (R = FAg-AuNCs/FSiNPs) toward other substances was similar to the blank and only exhibited significant decreases toward AChE (8.0 U/L) except for the biomolecules Cys and GSH. By introducing the special thiols scavenger N-ethylmaleimide (NEM) [6], the influences of GSH and Cys could be efficiently eliminated. As revealed in Fig. 7A, after being incubated with NEM, Cys and GSH exhibited insignificant influences on the fluorescence intensity ratio of the system. Furthermore, the anti-interference ability of the sensor was studied by adding these substances into the system with the presence of AChE (Fig. 7B). As shown in Fig. 7B, with the addition of these foreign substances, the system displayed the equal responses to AChE. These results suggested that the RF platform exhibited desirable recognition selectivity and satisfactory anti-interference capability for AChE activity determination.

Previous studies have reported that the activity of AChE in the human serum is widely considered as a biomarker for many clinical diseases, such as psychosis [57], myocardial infarction [58] and organophosphate poisoning [59]. Therefore, in this work, human serum was also selected as the model matrix to further assess the potential clinical application of the proposed RF strategy for AChE activity detection. Besides, as reveal in Fig. S7, the Ellman’s colorimetric method was adopted as a control method for detecting AChE activity in the human serum to validate the accuracy of this method [60]. Human serum sample was finally diluted 2000-fold for measuring AChE activity, so that the AChE activity in the tested sample was in the linear range of this RF platform. Fig. S8 showed that the 2000-time diluted human serum sample exhibited negligible fluorescence and had no influence on the fluorescence of SiNPs, suggesting that the human serum sample with adequate dilution could circumvent the background interference from the proteins with blue fluorescence. Table S3 represented the results for the analysis of AChE activity in the serum sample that were obtained by the standard addition method. As revealed in Table S3, the analytical results of AChE activity were consistent with those obtained by the Ellman’s method. Considering the dilution times of the human serum sample, the original activities of AChE in the undiluted human serum sample were determined to be 4160 U/L by this assay and 4280 U/L by the Ellman’s method, respectively, which were well closed to the previously reported AChE values in the clinical human serum samples [59]. Besides, Table S3 showed that the favorable recoveries of this method between 97.3 % and 109.3 % were gotten with the relative standard deviation (RSD) less than 4.0 %. These results clearly demonstrated the promising operability of this assay for accurately monitoring AChE activity in the clinical diagnostics.

In summary, the Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs nanohybrids were facilely fabricated by an electrostatic interaction-motivated self-assembly strategy. More importantly, by virtues of the superior stability of Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs nanohybrids and the self-calibration function of RF methods, an RF platform was ingeniously constructed for the sensitive analysis of AChE activity with favorable selectivity and anti-interference capability by employing the Ag-AuNCs@SiNPs as the probes. Additionally, this strategy can realize the RF sensing of the AChE activity in human serum with robust performance. Especially, based on the hydrolysate of AChE directly triggered fluorescence signal variation, this method successfully avoided the attendance of any fluorescence dyes or quenchers, greatly simplifying the experiment procedures and reducing the test cost. Hence, it is expected that this work can contribute to some new inspirations to construct RF probes for the biosensing applications and provide a greatly prospective alternative for the AChE-related medical research and clinical diagnosis.

Junyang Chen: Investigation. Mengjun Wang: Formal analysis. Lu Liu: Data curation. Xingguang Su: Methodology, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Writing - review & editing.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Mengke Wang is currently carrying out her Doctor's degree under the guidance of Professor Xingguang Su in Jilin University (China). Her research is mainly focused on the metal nanoclusters and quantum dots and their applications for biosensors.

Ning Li is currently carrying out her Doctor's degree under the guidance of Professor Xingguang Su in Jilin University (China). Her research is focused on the application of carbon dots in biomedicine.

Shun Wang is pursuing his PhD study under the guidance of Professor Ruren Xu in Jilin University (China). His interest is the synthesis of conjugated microporous polymers and their application on sensing.

Junyang Chen is pursuing his PhD study under the guidance of Professor Xingguang Su in Jilin University (China). His research is mainly focused on the synthesis of silicon nanoparticles and their application on sensing.

Mengjun Wang is pursuing her PhD study under the guidance of Professor Xingguang Su in Jilin University (China). Her research is mainly focused on the application in metal nanoclusters-based fluorescence sensor.

Lu Liu is currently carrying out her MS degree under the guidance of associate professor Zihan Lin in Changchun University of Science and Technology (China). Her research interest is the synthesis of novel metal nanoclusters for the sensor application.

Xingguang Su is a professor at the Department of Analytical Chemistry at the College of Chemistry, Jilin University. She received her MS degree from Jilin University (China) in 1992 and her PhD degree from Jilin University (China) in 1999. Her research focuses on the synthesis, characterization, functionalization and application of quantum dots and quantum dots-tagged microspheres in biomedicine.
